title: Configuration Management
tldr: Configuration Management (CM) is the practice of systematically managing and maintaining the desired state of system configurations — including software, services, files, and user accounts.
CM ensures consistency across environments, prevents configuration drift, enables repeatable deployments, and provides audit trails of all configuration changes.
Key principles: idempotency (same result regardless of how many times run), desired state declaration (specify what, not how), automation, and version control of all configuration.

CM falls into two categories: provisioning tools (Terraform, CloudFormation — create infrastructure) and configuration management tools (Ansible, Puppet, Chef — configure software on existing infrastructure).
laymanDefinition: Configuration management is like having a dedicated facilities team for a hotel. Each room must have fresh towels, working lights, and the correct furniture arrangement (desired state). If a guest moves a chair (configuration drift), the facilities team notices during inspection and restores it (convergence). The team has a master checklist (playbook/manifest) detailing exactly how each room type should look. When the hotel upgrades its furniture (software update), every room gets the new furniture within hours.

deepDive.heading: Idempotency and Desired State
deepDive.text: Idempotency: applying the same configuration multiple times produces the same result. No unintended side effects from repeated runs. Desired state declaration: you declare that nginx should be installed and running — the tool figures out whether to install, start, or do nothing. This contrasts with procedural scripts that might fail if a package is already installed. Idempotency enables safe, automated, repeated execution essential for CI/CD and self-healing.

deepDive.heading: Configuration Drift and Remediation
deepDive.text: Drift occurs when actual system state differs from the desired configuration. Causes: manual changes, emergency fixes, failed deployments, inconsistent patching. Detection: periodic CM runs (every 30 min), drift detection tools (CloudFormation, Terraform plan), compliance scanning (Inspec). Remediation: automated convergence (CM tool reapplies desired state), alerts on persistent drift, blameless postmortems to identify systemic causes.

deepDive.heading: Configuration Management Tools Comparison
deepDive.text: Ansible: agentless (SSH), YAML playbooks, easiest to start, good for ad-hoc and config management. Puppet: master-agent, declarative DSL, best for large-scale Linux fleets, roles/profiles pattern. Chef: master-agent, Ruby DSL, strong testing culture (Test Kitchen, Inspec, ChefSpec). SaltStack: fast remote execution, event-driven, Python-based. Choose based on team skills, scale, and architectural preferences.

deepDive.heading: CM Best Practices
deepDive.text: Version control all configuration code. Use a single source of truth for node definitions (CMDB, inventory). Implement code review for configuration changes. Test changes in non-production environments. Use CI/CD for configuration deployment. Monitor CM run success/failure rates. Use graduated rollouts for configuration changes (canary nodes first). Document configuration standards. Separate configuration from code (Hiera, data bags, external data sources).

deepDive.heading: Configuration Management Lifecycle
deepDive.text: Define: write configuration code (playbooks, manifests, recipes). Test: validate in development with Test Kitchen or containers. Deploy: promote through dev → staging → production. Monitor: track run status, drift detection, compliance reports. Audit: review configuration history and changes. Improve: incorporate feedback and requirements. The CM lifecycle mirrors the application development lifecycle — configuration is treated as software.

interviewAnswer: Configuration management is essential for maintaining consistent, reliable systems at scale. Choose the right tool based on your team's skills and infrastructure complexity. Ansible is simplest for agentless setup. Puppet excels at large-scale Linux management. Chef offers strong testing practices. Regardless of tool, version control everything, implement CI/CD, and monitor for drift and failures.

interviewQuestions.question: What is configuration management?
interviewQuestions.answer: The practice of systematically managing and maintaining desired system configuration state.

interviewQuestions.question: What is idempotency in configuration management?
interviewQuestions.answer: Running the same configuration multiple times produces the same result — no unintended side effects.

interviewQuestions.question: What is configuration drift?
interviewQuestions.answer: When actual system state differs from the desired configuration due to manual changes or failures.

interviewQuestions.question: What is the difference between provisioning and configuration management?
interviewQuestions.answer: Provisioning creates infrastructure (Terraform). Configuration management configures software on existing infrastructure (Ansible).

interviewQuestions.question: Is Ansible agentless?
interviewQuestions.answer: Yes — Ansible connects via SSH without installing agents on managed nodes.

interviewQuestions.question: What is an Ansible playbook?
interviewQuestions.answer: A YAML file defining configuration workflows — hosts, tasks, variables, and handlers.

interviewQuestions.question: What is a Puppet manifest?
interviewQuestions.answer: A .pp file using declarative DSL to define system resources and their desired state.

interviewQuestions.question: What is a Chef cookbook?
interviewQuestions.answer: A package of recipes, attributes, templates, and files for configuring a system component.

interviewQuestions.question: What is convergence in CM?
interviewQuestions.answer: The process of applying configuration to bring a system from its current state to the desired state.

interviewQuestions.question: Why should configuration be in version control?
interviewQuestions.answer: For audit trail, change tracking, collaboration, rollback capability, and CI/CD integration.

codeExamples.title: CM Drift Detection with Ansible
codeExamples.code:
# drift-check.yml — Detect configuration drift
---
- name: Check system state for drift
  hosts: all
  gather_facts: yes

  tasks:
    - name: Verify package state
      package:
        name: nginx
        state: present
      check_mode: yes  # Dry-run mode
      register: package_check

    - name: Verify service state
      service:
        name: nginx
        state: running
        enabled: yes
      check_mode: yes
      register: service_check

    - name: Report drift
      debug:
        msg: "DRIFT: {{ inventory_hostname }} has configuration drift"
      when: package_check.changed or service_check.changed
codeExamples.description: Ansible drift detection playbook using check_mode to verify system state without making changes.

codeExamples.title: Multi-Tool CM Strategy
codeExamples.code:
# Infrastructure Pipeline

# Step 1: Provision infrastructure with Terraform
$ terraform init
$ terraform plan -out=tfplan
$ terraform apply tfplan

# Step 2: Generate Ansible inventory from Terraform output
$ terraform output -json > /tmp/tf_output.json
$ cat /tmp/tf_output.json | jq -r '
  .instance_ips.value[] | select(.role == "web") | .private_ip
' > inventory/webservers.ini

# Step 3: Run Ansible for configuration
$ ansible-playbook -i inventory/webservers.ini \
  site.yml --limit webservers

# Step 4: Run Inspec compliance tests
$ inspec exec compliance_tests/ -t ssh://ubuntu@ip

# Step 5: Monitor ongoing CM runs
# Ansible Tower/AWX schedules periodic playbooks
# Puppet/Chef agents run every 30 minutes
codeExamples.description: Combined Terraform (provisioning) and Ansible (configuration) workflow for infrastructure automation.

codeExamples.title: CM Lifecycle in CI/CD
codeExamples.code:
# .github/workflows/config-management.yml
name: Configuration Management
on:
  push:
    paths: ["infra/**", "playbooks/**"]

jobs:
  lint:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v3
      - name: Lint Ansible playbooks
        run: ansible-lint playbooks/
      - name: Lint Terraform code
        run: terraform fmt -check infra/

  test:
    needs: lint
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Test playbooks (check mode)
        run: ansible-playbook playbooks/site.yml --check
      - name: Test Terraform plan
        run: terraform plan infra/

  deploy-canary:
    needs: test
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Apply to canary
        run: ansible-playbook playbooks/site.yml -l canary

  deploy-production:
    needs: deploy-canary
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Apply to production
        run: ansible-playbook playbooks/site.yml -l production
codeExamples.description: CI/CD pipeline for configuration management with linting, testing, canary, and production deployment stages.

mcqQuestions.question: What is idempotency?
options: Running multiple times produces same result
mcqQuestions.explanation: Idempotency means repeated application produces the same result without side effects.

mcqQuestions.question: What is configuration drift?
options: When actual state differs from desired configuration
mcqQuestions.explanation: Drift occurs when manual changes cause actual state to differ from desired state.

mcqQuestions.question: Which CM tool is agentless?
mcqQuestions.explanation: Ansible connects via SSH without requiring agents on managed nodes.

mcqQuestions.question: What is convergence in CM?
options: Applying configuration to reach desired state
mcqQuestions.explanation: Convergence is the process of applying configuration changes to reach desired state.

mcqQuestions.question: What is the difference between provisioning and CM?
options: Provisioning creates infra, CM configures software
CM creates infra, provisioning configures software
mcqQuestions.explanation: Terraform provisions, Ansible configures — they are complementary.

mcqQuestions.question: Why is version control important for CM?
mcqQuestions.explanation: Version control enables change tracking, review, rollback, and automated deployment of configuration.
